title: Depreciation: SL vs Units vs DDB
event: Reyes Co. buys a machine for $100,000 with a $10,000 salvage value, a 5-year life, and expected output of 45,000 units. In year 1 it produces 12,000 units. Compute year-1 depreciation under the assumed method.

computationPaths.narration: Straight-line spreads depreciable cost evenly across the life.
Units-of-production ties the expense to actual output.
DDB doubles the straight-line rate and applies it to BOOK VALUE — salvage is ignored in the formula but floors the ending book value.

memorize.body: SL = (cost − salvage) ÷ life. Units = (cost − salvage) ÷ total units × units produced. DDB = (2 ÷ life) × beginning BOOK VALUE.
DDB is the odd one out twice: it IGNORES salvage in the calculation and uses full cost (book value), not depreciable cost — but never depreciate below salvage.
All methods reach the same total depreciation over the life — they only argue about the timing.

questions.prompt: Cost $100,000, salvage $10,000, 5-year life. Year-1 DOUBLE-DECLINING depreciation = ?

distractors.feedback: You applied 40% to depreciable cost (90,000). DDB uses full BOOK VALUE in the formula: 40% × 100,000.
That's straight-line. DDB front-loads: double the SL rate on book value = 40,000.
